senses.definition: plural of potshard: a fragment of pottery or a broken piece of a pot
examples: The archaeologists found several potshards at the site.
Each potshard helped them reconstruct ancient vessels.

senses.definition: a group arrangement where potters share resources or workspace (rare; not common in modern usage)
examples: The potshare program allowed several artists to collaborate in a shared studio.
In the potshare, members split costs for kilns and glazes.

senses.definition: plural form of potshare; collective arrangement among potters
examples: The potshares allow members to pool materials.
Several potshares emerged in the craft district.

senses.definition: a variant spelling of potsheaf/potshaw (archaic or dialect term; not widely used in modern English)
examples: An old potshaw lay on the shelf in the cottage.
The tale mentioned a potshaw as part of rural kitchen gear.

senses.definition: a broken piece of pottery or ceramic material
examples: The farmer picked up a potsherd from the field.
Potsherds were scattered around the ancient ruin.

senses.definition: plural of potsherd
examples: The dig yielded many potsherds from several periods.
She studied the potsherds to learn about ancient pottery techniques.

senses.definition: an act of firing a gun from the potting or trap; or a slang term for a photo shoot involving pots (very rare)
examples: The term potshoot appeared in a quirky regional comic.
In vintage slang, a potshoot could mean a quick shot with a pepper pot.

senses.definition: a person who fires a gun from a potting or casual stance; slang usage; not common
examples: The old western novel mentions a notorious potshooter.
Local slang uses potshooter for a reckless shooter.

senses.definition: a shop selling pots or pottery; a pottery shop
examples: They visited a potshop to buy handmade bowls.
The potshop upstairs also offers pottery classes.

senses.definition: plural of potshop: shops that sell pots or pottery
examples: The market has several potshops selling handmade mugs.
Local potshops often host weekend demonstrations.

senses.definition: a shot fired at a distant target; also a casual or opportunistic shot taken at someone or something
examples: The politician took a potshot at his opponent during the debate.
The media ignored the real issue and focused on potshots.

senses.definition: to take a potshot: to shoot or attack in a casual or opportunistic way
examples: The columnist potshot at the company's ethics.
He potshots at rivals in every interview.

senses.definition: plural of potshot; casual or opportunistic attacks or shots
examples: The article contains several potshots at industry norms.
Potshots in interviews can backfire for a candidate.

senses.definition: the act of taking potshots; sniping or making casual attacks
examples: Potshotting during debates damaged his credibility.
Avoid potshotting at colleagues in reviews.

senses.definition: Australian or British slang for a person with a potbelly or a friend (informal; could be affectionate or pejorative depending on context)
examples: Cheers, potsie! How's your day going?
The old potsie never stopped bragging about his cooking.

senses.definition: plural of potsie
examples: The group of potsies gathered after the match.
Several potsies shared stories over coffee.

senses.definition: a type of plant stem or a cooking utensil; informal term may refer to the sticky part of a pot or a malfunctioning pot handling (rare)
examples: The chef wiped the potstick to avoid burning.
A potstick can scatter when hot.

senses.definition: a type of dumpling, often pan-fried and steamed, common in East Asian cuisine
examples: We ordered potstickers as an appetizer.
Potstickers are crispy on the bottom and tender on top.

senses.definition: plural of potsticker
examples: Potstickers are served with soy sauce.
The restaurant specializes in potstickers and noodles.

senses.definition: a stone used to place pots on or a type of stone associated with pots (rare; not common in modern usage)
examples: The potstone was smooth from years of use.
They placed the kettle on the potstone to cool.

senses.definition: plural of potstone
examples: Old potstones lay along the hearth.
Collectors seek ancient potstones with inscriptions.

senses.definition: slang or affectionate term for a person with potbelly or friend; similar to potsie
examples: Hey potsy, long time no see.
The nickname potsy stuck with him at the reunion.

senses.definition: a rapid movement or sprint (dialectal/rare), or a surname; not common as a standalone widely used term
examples: A pott of the horse startled the rider.
In dialect, pott can refer to a quick movement.

senses.definition: capable of being put into a pot; suitable for cooking or potting
examples: Not all herbs are pottable in a single pot.
The chef looked for pottable ingredients for the stew.

senses.definition: a thick soup or stew, usually made from vegetables and grains; an old-fashioned or historical dish
examples: The travelers ate pottage by the fire.
Pottage was a staple in medieval kitchens.

senses.definition: plural of pottage
examples: The cookbook features several pottages from different cultures.
Ancient households often relied on pottages.
